summary: The Delhi Sultanate (1206-1526) was a succession of Muslim dynasties ruling northern India from the city of Delhi, founded when the Ghurid general Qutb ud-Din Aibak established the Mamluk (Slave) Dynasty after conquests by Muhammad of Ghor. Five dynasties ruled in turn -- the Mamluk/Slave, Khalji, Tughlaq, Sayyid, and Lodi -- through rulers including the only female sultan of the Indian subcontinent, Razia Sultana, and the Mongol-repelling Alauddin Khalji. Muhammad bin Tughluq briefly moved the capital to Daulatabad and survived Timur's 1398 sack of Delhi, while the traveler Ibn Battuta served as a judge at the Tughlaq court. The Sultanate's final Lodi Dynasty fell when Ibrahim Lodi was defeated by Babur at the First Battle of Panipat in 1526, giving way to the Mughal Empire.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: The Delhi Sultanate is identified in giveaways as the polity named for the North Indian city of Delhi, ruled successively by dynasties including the Tughlaq and Lodi, and eventually succeeded by the Mughal Empire after the First Battle of Panipat.

clues.clue: Named for the North Indian city of Delhi, its capital; ruled by dynasties such as the Tughlaq and Lodi
examples: name this polity named for a North Indian city.
name this sultanate ruled by dynasties such as the Tughlaq and Lodi, named for a northern Indian city.

clues.clue: Succeeded by the Mughal Empire, which was later ruled by the Lodi dynasty before that
examples: the Mughals succeeded what Islamic sultanate that was ruled from a namesake city in northern India?
what empire was later ruled by the Lodi dynasty from its namesake city?

clues.clue: Ruled from what is now the capital of India
examples: name this sultanate that mostly ruled from the present-day capital of India.

clues.clue: Ruling dynasties included the Burji and Khilji [Khalji]
examples: This polity's ruling dynasties included the Burji and Khilji, and rulers of this polity including Balban were former slave soldiers known as ghulams.

clues.clue: Descended from conquests made by Mahmud of Ghor [Muhammad of Ghor], and was an enemy of the Rajputs; the collective name covers the Mamluk, Khalji, Tughlaq, Sayyid, and Lodi dynasties that ruled from the thirteenth to sixteenth centuries
examples: They descended from conquests made by Mahmud of Ghor, and were enemies of the Rajputs.
give the common collective name for these Muslim kingdoms, including the Khalki, Sayyid, and Lodi dynasties, which ruled from the eleventh to sixteenth centuries from a certain Indian city.

clues.clue: Established as a result of the Battles of Taraori and Chandawar and other campaigns of Qutb al-Din Aibak
examples: One of these governments was set up as a result of the battle of Taraori and Chandawar, and other campaigns of Qutb al-Din Aibak.

clues.clue: Briefly re-established by Sher Shah Suri after Ibrahim [Lodi] fell to Babur at Panipat
examples: The last of these states was briefly re-established by Sher Shah after its ruler Ibrahim had fallen to Babur at Panipat.

works.name: Founding: Ghurid Conquest & the Mamluk (Slave) Dynasty
works.description: The Sultanate began when the Ghurid general Qutb ud-Din Aibak, a former slave (mamluk) of Muhammad of Ghor, established the Mamluk or "Slave" Dynasty in 1206, first ruling from Lahore before the capital shifted to Delhi. His descendant Iltutmish stabilized the dynasty and named his daughter Razia Sultana as heir; she became the only female Muslim ruler of the Indian subcontinent before being overthrown amid rumors of a romance with the Abyssinian slave Jamal ud-Din Yaqut. The dynasty's later rule was checked by the Corps of Forty, a council of Turkic slave-nobles eventually destroyed by the sultan Balban.

clues.clue: Founded in 1206 by Qutb ud-Din Aibak, a former slave general of the Ghurid dynasty honored by the Qutb Minar he began building; the dynasty's first capital was Lahore
examples: The Qutb Minar's construction was started by this state's founder, Qutb-ud-Din Aibak.
A general who had served the Ghurids, Qutb al-Din Aibak, founded this polity, making Lahore its first capital.

clues.clue: Stabilized by Iltutmish, who named his daughter Razia as his heir (having assumed power after the fraught rule of a rival regent)
examples: yet another ruler, Iltumish, appointed his daughter Raziya as his heir.
One of these political entities was stabilised by Ghiyas al-Din Balban following the fraught rule of Iltutmish.

clues.clue: Razia Sultana seized power by donning a red garment (kaghzi pirhan) symbolizing seekers of justice and inciting the public against her stepmother Shah Turkan, who had assumed control during her brother Rukn al-Din Firuz's reign
examples: A ruler of this polity seized power by donning a red garment or kaghzi pirhan, which symbolised seekers of justice, before inciting the public against their stepmother.
ShahTurkan, who assumed control during the reign of her son Rukn al-Din Firuz, was overthrown by this polity's only female ruler Razia.

clues.clue: Razia Sultana referenced the Queen of Sheba on coins inscribed "Bilqis-i Jihan"; a contemporary wrote that "good fortune turned its back" when it saw "black dust" on her clothing
examples: One contemporary wrote that "good fortune turned its back" when it "saw black dust" on the clothing of that ruler of this polity, who referenced a legendary predecessor on coins inscribed with "Bilqis-i Jihan".

clues.clue: Razia Sultana was overthrown amid a rumored romance with the Abyssinian (Habshi) slave Jamal ud-Din Yaqut
examples: A rumored romance with an Abyssinian slave led to the overthrow of this polity's queen Razia Sultana.
One of the earliest powerful Habshi was Jamal ud-Din Yaqut, an advisor and possible lover of the only female ruler of this state, Razia Sultan.

clues.clue: Administered by a council of Turkic slave-nobles called the Corps of Forty, destroyed by the sultan Balban
examples: It's not Venice, but this polity was administered by a council of slaves called the Corps of Forty until it was destroyed by Balban.

works.name: Khalji Dynasty
works.description: The Khalji Dynasty, which came to power via the Khalji Revolution after the inept Qaiqabad succeeded (and Bughra Khan declined the throne to keep his own governorship), was defined by the aggressive rule of Alauddin Khalji. Alauddin repeatedly repelled invasions by the Chagatai Khanate -- his general Zafar Khan died winning the Battle of Kili -- financed his standing army by raising the khums tax on war booty to 80% and the kharaj tax on peasant produce to 50%, and imposed price controls and anti-hoarding rules through the Shahana-i-Mandi and Diwan-i-Riyasat systems. His enslaved general Malik Kafur briefly seized the throne and attempted to convert the sultanate to his own religion before being swiftly overthrown.

clues.clue: Bughra Khan refused the leadership to keep his own governorship; his inept son Qaiqabad became ruler instead and fell to the Khalji Revolution
examples: Bughra Khan refused leadership of this state to keep his governorship; his inept son Qaiqabad became ruler instead and fell to the Khalji Revolution.

clues.clue: The enslaved general Malik Kafur briefly seized the throne, attempted to convert the sultanate to his own religion, and was swiftly overthrown; the historian Barani was greatly critical of him
examples: One ruler of this polity was the former slave Malik Kafur, who attempted to convert this polity to his own religion and was swiftly overthrown.
This polity's historian Barani was greatly critical of the captured former slave Malik Kafur, who briefly ruled it.

clues.clue: Alauddin Khalji fixed prices and banned hoarding through the Shahana-i-Mandi and Diwan-i-Riyasat systems
examples: A ruler of this state fixed prices and banned hoarding with the Shahana-i-Mandi and Diwan-i-Riyasat systems.

clues.clue: Raised the khums tax on war booty to 80% and the kharaj tax on peasant produce to 50% to fund the fight against invasions, using the revenue to build the royal water tank Hauz Khas
examples: To help fight off invasions, this empire raised the khums tax on war booty to 80% and the kharaj tax on peasant produce to a crushing 50%; it then used that revenue to build its royal water tank, the Hauz Khas.

clues.clue: General Zafar Khan died at the Battle of Kili, which repulsed the last of a series of Chagatai Khanate invasions
examples: This empire's general Zafar Khan died at the Battle of Kili, at which it repulsed the last of a series of invasions by the great tumens of the Chagatai Khanate.

clues.clue: Ruled by the Mongol-repelling Alauddin Khalji
examples: The Mongol-defeating Alauddin Khilji ruled this empire, which survived the sack of its capital by Timur in 1398.

works.name: Tughlaq Dynasty
works.description: Under the Tughlaq Dynasty the Sultanate reached its greatest territorial extent under Muhammad bin Tughluq, who briefly relocated the capital to Daulatabad in the Deccan before returning it to Delhi. The traveler Ibn Battuta served for six years as a judge at his court. Later Tughlaq rulers expanded Delhi's urban core -- building the Siri Fort complex and the district of Firozabad among the city's "seven older cities" -- before the dynasty's decline culminated in Timur's sack of the capital in 1398.

clues.clue: Reached its greatest extent under Muhammad bin Tughluq
examples: Razia Sultan ruled this state, which reached its greatest extent under Muhammad bin Tughluq.

clues.clue: Briefly moved its capital to Daulatabad
examples: This polity briefly moved its capital to Daulatabad, and its final period of expansion occurred under Sultan Sikandar Lodi, one of whose descendants lost the First Battle of Panipat.
One state known under this general name set up an auxiliary capital at Daulatabad, only to see the Bahmanis secede from its rule.

clues.clue: Muhammad bin Tughluq employed the traveler Ibn Battuta as a judge (qadi) for six years
examples: Ibn Battuta paused his travels for six years while serving as a judge under this sultanate's ruler Muhammad bin Tughluq.

clues.clue: Historian Ziauddin Barani describes how the empire's rulers built four of the so-called "seven older cities" of the capital, including the Siri Fort complex and the district of Firozabad
examples: The historian Ziauddin Barani describes how this empire's rulers built four of the so-called "seven older cities" of its capital, including the Siri Fort complex and the district of Firozabad.

clues.clue: Survived the sack of its capital by Timur in 1398
examples: The Mongol-defeating Alauddin Khilji ruled this empire, which survived the sack of its capital by Timur in 1398.

works.name: Sayyid & Lodi Dynasties
works.description: After Khizr Khan founded the short-lived Sayyid Dynasty by falsely claiming descent from Muhammad, the Sayyids were overthrown by the Afghan Lodi Dynasty, whose first ruler Bahlul touched off a succession crisis by naming his son Babrak viceroy. Bahlul's successor Sikandar Lodi -- who wrote poetry under the pseudonym Gulrukhi and destroyed an idol at the Nagarkot temple -- founded Agra as an alternate capital and conquered Gwalior Fort from the Tomar dynasty. The dynasty's final ruler, Ibrahim Lodi, was defeated and killed at the First Battle of Panipat in 1526 by Babur, ending the Sultanate and beginning the Mughal Empire.

clues.clue: Khizr Khan rose to power by falsely claiming descent from Muhammad, establishing the short-lived Sayyid Dynasty of "sayyids," which was overthrown by the Lodi family
examples: In this polity, Khizr Khan rose in status by falsely claiming to be descended from Muhammad, establishing a dynasty of such sayyids.
This family overthrew the short-lived Sayyid Dynasty that had been founded by Kizir Khan.

clues.clue: The Lodi Dynasty's first ruler, Bahlul, created a succession crisis by naming his son Babrak as viceroy
examples: Its first ruler, Bahlul, created a succession crisis when he named his son Babrak as viceroy.

clues.clue: Sikandar Lodi, who wrote poetry under the pseudonym Gulrukhi, founded the city of Agra as an alternate capital
examples: This state's penultimate ruler wrote poetry under the pseudonym Gulrukhi.
This dynasty founded the modern city of Agra as an alternate capital for its second ruler, Sikandar.

clues.clue: Sikandar Lodi destroyed an idol at the Nagarkot temple and gave its pieces to butchers to use as weights, and took Gwalior Fort from the Tomar dynasty after the death of Maan Singh
examples: A ruler of this dynasty destroyed an idol at the Nagarkot temple and gave its pieces to butchers to be used as weights.
The Tomar dynasty lost Gwalior Fort to this state after the death of Maan Singh.

clues.clue: The final ruler, Ibrahim Lodi, was defeated and killed at the First Battle of Panipat by Babur, ending the Sultanate
examples: This sultanate's last ruler, Ibrahim Lodi, was defeated at the First Battle of Panipat by Babur.
name this final ruling family of the Delhi Sultanate, which was defeated at the First Battle of Panipat after its leader Ibrahim was betrayed to the Mughals.

clues.clue: The Lodis were the Sultanate's last dynasty, conquered by Babur and the Mughals in the 1526 Battle of Panipat
examples: This polity's last dynasty, the Lodis, was conquered by Babur and the Mughals.

works.name: Administration, Society & Culture
works.description: The Sultanate's Muslim conquerors called themselves ashraf ("sons of Muhammad") and stratified society by sorting subjects into ashraf and ajlaf classes, a system described by the historian Ziya ud-Din Barani in his Fatawa-ye Jahandari. Courtly culture included the poet Amir Khusrau, employed by one ruler, while the Persian traveler and historian's accounts (Barani's chronicles) remain the primary source for the dynasty's internal politics.

clues.clue: The historian Ziya ud-Din Barani's Fatawa-ye Jahandari advocated sorting citizens into the ashraf and ajlaf classes
examples: The Fatawa-ye Jahandari, written in this polity by Ziya ud-Din Barani, advocated for its citizens to be sorted into the ashraf and ajlaf classes.

The Muslim conquerors who established this sultanate in the 13th century referred to themselves as ashraf, or sons of Muhammad, and eventually split into groups loosely aligned with the local caste system.

clues.clue: One ruler employed the poet Amir Khusrau
examples: Another ruler of this polity employed the poet Amir Khusrau

works.name: Legacy & Neighboring Powers
works.description: The Sultanate repeatedly clashed with and raided neighboring Indian kingdoms: Alauddin Khalji's forces looted the Hoysala capital of Halebidu and besieged the Pandya stronghold of Madurai, and Sultanate raids weakened the Reddi kingdom and hastened the decline of the Pandya dynasty. Its repeated invasions helped bring down the Hoysala Empire, whose remnants fed into the rise of the southern Vijayanagara Empire, which resisted Sultanate control in the south for much of its existence.

clues.clue: Forces under Alauddin Khalji looted the Hoysala capital of Halebidu and besieged the Pandya stronghold of Madurai
examples: Halebidu was looted, and the Pandya stronghold of Madurai besieged, by the forces of Alauddin Khilji, a ruler of the Muslim sultanate based in this Indian city, whose rulers included the Tugluq and Lodi families.

clues.clue: Repeated invasions helped collapse the Hoysala Empire, whose remnants gave rise to the Vijayanagara Empire, which resisted Sultanate control in the south for much of its existence
examples: Vijayanagara grew out of the ashes of the Hoysala Empire, which collapsed under the repeated invasions of this northern sultanate centered at the location of modern-day India's capital.
The Khalji Dynasty ruled over this sultanate, which was also ruled by the Tughlaq and Lodi dynasties. For much of its existence, the Vijayanagara Empire resisted its control in south India.

clues.clue: Sultanate raids weakened the Reddi kingdom and exacerbated the 14th-century decline of the Pandya dynasty
examples: The Reddi kingdom managed to resist invasions by the long-lived sultanate named for this city, which managed to repulse Mongol invasions.
The decline of the Pandya dynasty in the 14th century was exacerbated by raids ordered by a sultanate based in this city.

comprehensive_summary: The Delhi Sultanate (1206-1526) was a succession of five Muslim dynasties ruling northern India from the city of Delhi. It began when Qutb ud-Din Aibak, a former slave (mamluk) general of the Ghurid conqueror Muhammad of Ghor, founded the Mamluk or "Slave" Dynasty in 1206, starting construction of the Qutb Minar and first ruling from Lahore. His successor Iltutmish stabilized the dynasty and named his daughter Razia Sultana as heir; she seized power by donning a red garment symbolizing seekers of justice and became the only female Muslim ruler of the Indian subcontinent, referencing the Queen of Sheba on her coinage, before being overthrown amid rumors of a romance with the Abyssinian slave Jamal ud-Din Yaqut. The Mamluk sultan Balban later destroyed the Corps of Forty, a council of Turkic slave-nobles that had checked royal power. The Khalji Dynasty followed, dominated by Alauddin Khalji, who repeatedly repelled Chagatai Khanate invasions (his general Zafar Khan dying at the Battle of Kili), funded a standing army by raising the khums tax on war booty to 80% and the kharaj tax on peasants to 50%, and imposed price controls through the Shahana-i-Mandi and Diwan-i-Riyasat systems; his enslaved general Malik Kafur briefly seized the throne before being swiftly overthrown. The Tughlaq Dynasty brought the Sultanate to its greatest extent under Muhammad bin Tughluq, who briefly moved the capital to Daulatabad and employed the traveler Ibn Battuta as a judge for six years; later Tughlaq rulers expanded Delhi's urban core with the Siri Fort complex and Firozabad before Timur sacked the capital in 1398. Khizr Khan then founded the short-lived Sayyid Dynasty by falsely claiming descent from Muhammad, which was overthrown by the Afghan Lodi Dynasty. Lodi ruler Sikandar (who wrote poetry as "Gulrukhi") founded Agra as an alternate capital and conquered Gwalior Fort from the Tomar dynasty, before the Sultanate's final ruler, Ibrahim Lodi, was defeated and killed by Babur at the First Battle of Panipat in 1526, ending the Sultanate and beginning the Mughal Empire (Sher Shah Suri briefly revived it afterward). Society under the Sultanate's Muslim rulers, who called themselves ashraf ("sons of Muhammad"), was stratified into ashraf and ajlaf classes as described by the historian Ziya ud-Din Barani in his Fatawa-ye Jahandari; courtly culture included the poet Amir Khusrau. The Sultanate's invasions helped collapse the southern Hoysala Empire (whose remnants fed the rise of the Vijayanagara Empire, which long resisted Sultanate control) and weakened the Reddi kingdom and the Pandya dynasty.
recursive_suggestions: Mamluk Sultanate (of Egypt) -- surfaced as its own answerline (T2) that explicitly rejects Delhi Sultanate as an answer; a separate slave-soldier state ruling Egypt, excluded entirely
Ghurid Dynasty -- Muhammad of Ghor's Afghanistan-based dynasty whose general founded the Delhi Sultanate; own answerline (B5, B10); excluded, referenced in the founding section

Razia Sultana -- surfaced as her own answerline (B5 Part 3); the Sultanate's only female ruler is thoroughly covered here via Delhi-answer clues, but could support a standalone page if clue density grows
Vijayanagara Empire -- rival/successor southern empire, own answerline (B1, B7); excluded, referenced within the Legacy & Neighboring Powers section
Hoysala Empire / Pandya dynasty -- southern Indian polities weakened by Sultanate invasions, own answerlines (B11, B3); excluded, referenced briefly
Ibn Battuta -- Moroccan traveler who served as a judge under Muhammad bin Tughluq; own answerline (B6 Part 2); excluded, referenced within the Tughlaq Dynasty section
Khalji Dynasty and Lodi Dynasty -- surfaced with independent answerlines (B8 Part 1; T8) rich enough to eventually support their own pages; currently folded into this page as dynastic-phase sections
Mughal Empire -- the Sultanate's successor state after the First Battle of Panipat (1526); own extensive answerline, excluded
Ambiguity flag: 'Delhi sultanates' (plural, T9) is a near-synonymous collective-name answerline for the same topic; its clues were folded into the General section here
